Chapter 3: ESG Fund Flows and Performance Reality — The Gap Between Marketing and Returns

Research Date: July 2026 Searches Conducted: 25+ independent queries Sources Cited: 30+ primary sources Confidence Level: High Temporal Caveat: Research current as of July 2026.

Opening: The Trillion-Dollar Mirage

In 2021, the investment industry promised a green revolution. Bloomberg Intelligence projected that ESG assets would reach $53 trillion by 2025 — representing roughly one-third of all assets under management globally 1. PwC predicted $34 trillion by 2026. Broadridge forecast $30 trillion by 2030. These figures were not idle speculation; they appeared in pitch decks, board presentations, and regulatory filings. They drove hiring sprees, product launches, and C-suite restructuring at the world’s largest asset managers.

The reality? Global sustainable fund assets reached approximately $3.56 trillion at the close of 2024 and expanded to just $3.9 trillion by the end of 2025 2. The industry missed its most conservative projection by nearly $26 trillion — a gap larger than the combined GDP of the world’s 20 smallest economies.

What happened between the promise and the reality is not merely a story of market cycles. It is a story of statistical sleight-of-hand, marketing inflation, survivorship bias, and systematic greenwashing exposed by regulatory enforcement. This chapter documents the mechanics of the ESG fund performance myth — how the gap between marketing and returns was constructed, maintained, and ultimately began to collapse.

1. Global ESG AUM: The $3.9 Trillion Reality vs. the $53 Trillion Promise

1.1 The Projection Gap

The divergence between projected and actual ESG assets under management (AUM) represents one of the most significant forecasting failures in modern financial history.

Projection Source Projected ESG AUM Target Year Actual (End of 2025) Shortfall
Bloomberg Intelligence 1 $53 trillion 2025 $3.9 trillion $49.1 trillion
PwC $34 trillion 2026 $3.9 trillion $30.1 trillion
Broadridge 1 $30 trillion 2030 $3.9 trillion $26.1 trillion

This is not a minor variance. It is a 92.6% miss against the Bloomberg projection — the equivalent of forecasting a hurricane and getting a drizzle.

1.2 The AUM Definition Game: How the Industry Inflated Its Numbers

The projection gap reveals a critical sleight of hand in industry reporting. Industry projections typically counted any fund that considered ESG factors in any capacity — even if ESG was a minor input among dozens of investment criteria 2.

Bloomberg Intelligence’s 2021 projection of $53 trillion 1 was based on a broad definition that included any institutional mandate mentioning ESG — even if ESG was a peripheral consideration. By contrast, when counting only dedicated sustainable funds with explicit ESG mandates, the figure was far smaller. Dedicated sustainable funds represent less than 7% of global AUM 2.

The industry also conflated European SFDR Article 8 and Article 9 classifications with genuine ESG integration. European Article 8/9 classified vehicles reached more than $10 trillion 2, but these classifications include funds with minimal ESG differentiation — Article 8, in particular, captures any fund that merely “promotes” environmental or social characteristics, a threshold so low that conventional funds can qualify with minor disclosure adjustments.

The Mechanism of Inflation: 1. Count every fund that mentions ESG in its prospectus, regardless of actual weight 2. Include Article 8 funds that require minimal ESG integration 3. Aggregate across all asset classes including fixed income and alternatives where ESG definitions are loosest 4. Project compound growth rates from a inflated baseline 5. Present the projection as “ESG assets” without definitional caveats

2. ESG Fund Performance vs. Traditional Funds: The Academic Verdict

2.1 The Outperformance Claim

For nearly a decade, the asset management industry promoted ESG investing as a source of superior risk-adjusted returns — “alpha” in industry parlance. Marketing materials from BlackRock, State Street, and Vanguard routinely cited studies suggesting that ESG-conscious portfolios delivered better performance while reducing risk.

The claim had intuitive appeal: companies with strong governance, environmental stewardship, and social responsibility should, logically, be better-managed enterprises. Better-managed enterprises should outperform.

2.2 The Academic Evidence: No Reliable Alpha

A comprehensive review by the Fraser Institute (2024) found “no reliable statistical relationship between ESG-focused portfolios and above-average risk-adjusted returns” 3. The review examined multiple peer-reviewed studies, meta-analyses, and industry reports, concluding that claims of systematic ESG outperformance do not survive rigorous statistical scrutiny.

Northern Trust research similarly found that “ESG funds do not perform better or worse than other managed non-ESG funds on a risk-adjusted basis” 4. This finding — neither outperformance nor underperformance — is itself devastating to the marketing narrative, as ESG funds carry higher complexity and fee structures that should, in efficient markets, be compensated.

Stanford Graduate School of Business research concluded that when controlling for known investment factors and removing retroactive biases, ESG outperformance “largely disappears” 3.

The Financial Times reported that “ESG outperformance disappears when adjusted for risk, sector bias, and quality factors” 4. In other words, ESG funds that appeared to outperform were typically just overweight technology stocks (which tend to have higher ESG scores due to their low-carbon business models) during a period when tech stocks surged. When researchers controlled for sector concentration, the apparent advantage vanished.

2.3 2023–2024 Performance Data: The Narrowing Gap

The performance data tells a story of declining advantage:

2023: Morgan Stanley reported sustainable funds posted a median return of 12.6%, outpacing traditional funds’ 8.6% 5

2024: The outperformance advantage narrowed significantly — sustainable funds modestly outperformed by just 0.6 percentage points in H1 2024 5

Academic adjustment: After adjusting for industry factors, ESG funds tracked benchmarks very closely with no statistically significant difference 5

The pattern is clear: as ESG fund AUM grew, any early-mover advantage was arbitraged away. By 2024, the performance differential had narrowed to a statistical rounding error — while ESG funds continued to charge higher fees for their supposed differentiation.

3. The “ESG Outperformance” Myth: How Bias Created an Illusion

3.1 Survivorship Bias: The Dead Funds Don’t Tell Tales

Early ESG indices and funds suffer from survivorship bias — retrospective analysis only evaluated firms that survived to become highly rated, excluding bankruptcies and failing “brown” firms 3.

The Mechanism: When index providers constructed ESG indices looking backward, they selected companies that had already survived — companies with strong balance sheets, diversified operations, and competent management. These companies tended to have better financial performance and better ESG scores. But the causality ran from financial health to ESG scores, not the reverse. Bankrupt coal companies, failed chemical manufacturers, and shuttered fossil fuel firms never appeared in the ESG indices because they no longer existed to be rated.

3.2 Selection and Reporting Bias: The Rating Agency Problem

The ESG outperformance narrative was further distorted by systematic biases in how ESG data is collected and reported:

ESG Rating Divergence: Different ESG rating agencies assign drastically different scores to the same company due to lack of standardized metrics 3. A company can receive a top-quartile rating from one agency and a bottom-quartile rating from another. This divergence means that any researcher constructing an ESG portfolio has enormous discretion in which “ESG” companies to include — discretion that can be exercised to select companies with strong recent financial performance.

Geographic and Linguistic Bias: English-language media sources are disproportionately represented in ESG controversy data, skewing ratings against non-North American companies 3. A European or Asian firm with identical practices may receive a worse ESG score simply because its controversies are reported in English-language media more frequently.

Reverse Causality: Companies with strong financial performance can selectively report ESG initiatives — what researchers call “reverse causality” 3. Financial success enables investment in sustainability programs, diversity initiatives, and governance improvements. The financial success causes higher ESG scores, not vice versa.

3.3 The Critical Insight: A Wealth Transfer Mechanism

Even if ESG assets trade at a premium (lowering expected returns for investors), this premium lowers the cost of capital for ESG-compliant corporations. The ESG ecosystem thus functions as a wealth transfer from investors (who accept lower returns) to corporations (who pay less for capital), with intermediaries extracting fees at every step 5.

The investor pays a premium for ESG-labeled funds. The fund invests in ESG-scored companies. Those companies enjoy a lower cost of capital. But the investor does not capture the value created — the companies do. The intermediaries — fund managers, rating agencies, consultants — capture fees throughout.

4. The Great ESG Exodus: Fund Outflows 2022–2025

4.1 The Outflow Avalanche

Despite headline AUM growth (driven primarily by market appreciation rather than net inflows), ESG funds experienced significant and sustained net outflows starting in 2022:

Full-year 2025: $84 billion in net outflows globally, a dramatic reversal from $38 billion in inflows in 2024 6

Q3 2025 alone: $55.1 billion in net outflows, which Morningstar characterized as a “largely technical movement” 7

Persistent headwinds: Political backlash, greenwashing concerns, and regulatory uncertainty drove consistent outflows through 2024–2025 6

The 2025 outflows represent the largest annual exodus from sustainable funds since the category was formally tracked. An asset class that had been marketed as the inevitable future of finance was hemorrhaging capital at a rate of $7 billion per month.

4.2 Four Drivers of the Exodus

1. Political Backlash: Anti-ESG sentiment in the United States, particularly from Republican state attorneys general and pension fund divestment mandates, created an overtly hostile political environment. States including Texas, Florida, and Louisiana enacted legislation restricting state pension funds from considering ESG factors in investment decisions 6.

2. Performance Disappointment: Clean energy sectors underperformed in the high interest rate environment of 2022–2023. Renewable energy projects, with their capital-intensive, long-duration cash flows, are particularly sensitive to interest rate increases. ESG funds overweight in clean energy suffered accordingly 6.

3. Greenwashing Fatigue: Investors became skeptical of funds claiming genuine ESG differentiation. Multiple enforcement actions by the SEC and other regulators exposed the gap between marketing and reality, eroding trust in the ESG label itself 8.

4. Regulatory Complexity: Stricter naming rules and disclosure requirements — including ESMA’s fund naming guidelines in Europe and the SEC’s Names Rule in the United States — increased compliance costs and forced fund managers to either substantiate their ESG claims or abandon the label 9.

4.3 What the Outflows Reveal

The outflow data is revealing precisely because it contradicts a core ESG marketing thesis: that investor demand for sustainable investing is secular and irreversible. If ESG investing genuinely delivered both superior returns and positive impact, the outflows would not have occurred — or at minimum, would have been offset by inflows from institutional investors with long-term sustainability mandates.

Instead, the data suggests that much of the “demand” for ESG funds was cyclical and marketing-driven — investors bought ESG funds when they outperformed (during the tech-heavy rally of 2020–2021) and sold them when performance normalized and political headwinds intensified.

5. The Great Rebranding: 1,450+ Funds Quietly Drop “ESG” from Their Names

5.1 The Most Visible Sign of Retreat

The most visible indicator of ESG’s retreat is the wave of fund rebrandings that swept across Europe beginning in 2024. Under ESMA’s new naming guidelines, funds using ESG terms must apply strict minimum standards — including 80% investment thresholds and fossil fuel exclusions 9.

The Rebranding Statistics are Stark: - At least 1,450 European funds have rebranded or dropped sustainability-related terms from their names 9 - This represents up to 31% of the in-scope European fund universe 9 - Passive funds account for nearly 75% of funds that dropped ESG terms entirely 9 - Use of the specific term “ESG” in fund names plummeted by 24% 9 - The term “sustainable” fell by roughly 21% 9

5.2 The Pivot to Softer Language

Funds did not abandon ESG marketing — they simply adopted softer, less regulated terminology: - “Transition” funds grew by 15% 9 - “Governance” funds surged by 40% 9

These terms carry no regulatory threshold and require no specific ESG integration. A “transition” fund can hold fossil fuel companies claiming they are “transitioning.” A “governance” fund applies traditional corporate governance screens that have existed for decades, predating the ESG label entirely.

5.3 What the Rebrandings Reveal

The critical insight from the rebranding wave is this: fund managers didn’t change their investment strategies — they changed their names. Many “rebranded” funds retained identical portfolios but removed “ESG” or “sustainable” to avoid strict compliance thresholds 9. This suggests that a significant portion of “ESG investing” was marketing differentiation rather than genuine strategy differentiation.

When regulators required funds to substantiate their ESG claims with minimum investment thresholds and exclusion criteria, nearly a third of the industry preferred to drop the label entirely rather than alter their portfolios. This is not the behavior of managers who believe ESG integration enhances investment outcomes. It is the behavior of managers who recognized that the ESG label was primarily a marketing tool.

6. Greenwashing in Fund Marketing: SEC Enforcement Exposes the Gap

6.1 Invesco: $17.5 Million — ESG Integration That Wasn’t

In November 2024, the SEC charged Invesco Advisers with making misleading statements about how much of the firm’s investments were managed using ESG strategies, resulting in a $17.5 million penalty — one of the largest ESG-related fines in SEC history 8.

Between April 2020 and July 2022, Invesco’s marketing materials claimed that between 70% and 94% of its parent company’s funds were managed in a way that integrated ESG factors. However, the SEC found these percentages were inflated because they included “a substantial amount of assets” held in passive ETFs that did not consider ESG factors in investment decisions 8.

The internal motivation was explicit: by fall 2019, Invesco had an internal analysis showing that clients representing “at least $370 billion” in assets were at risk of moving to another firm. Believing that integrating ESG considerations “was of commercial importance,” Invesco accelerated its ESG integration claims — but notably, the firm “lacked any written policy defining ESG integration” 8.

“Invesco saw commercial value in claiming that a high percentage of company-wide assets were ESG integrated. But saying it doesn’t make it so.” — Sanjay Wadhwa, Acting Director, SEC Division of Enforcement 8

6.2 WisdomTree: $4 Million — Funds That Held Fossil Fuels and Tobacco

In October 2024, the SEC charged WisdomTree Asset Management with misstatements regarding the investment strategy of three ESG-marketed ETFs, resulting in a $4 million civil penalty 10.

The funds’ prospectuses stated that WisdomTree’s investment model would exclude securities of companies involved in fossil fuels and tobacco “regardless of revenue measures.” However, because WisdomTree contracted with third-party data vendors whose data sets did not screen for ESG in a manner consistent with the prospectus claims, each of the funds held securities of companies involved in both fossil fuel production and tobacco 10.

Critically, the SEC found that WisdomTree “did not adopt or implement any policies and procedures for its ESG investment process” — there was no compliance infrastructure to ensure the funds actually did what their prospectuses claimed 10.

At the time of their “ESG” rebranding in 2020, the three funds collectively had only $138 million in assets. By 2024, all three had liquidated 10. The penalty — nearly 3% of peak AUM — demonstrates that the SEC will pursue seven-figure fines even for small funds with modest assets.

6.3 The Regulatory Message

Both firms settled with the SEC without admitting or denying the findings. These actions proved that the SEC’s crackdown on ESG-related misstatements remained highly active even after the agency formally disbanded its specialized Climate and ESG Task Force in late 2024 8.

The SEC’s Division of Examinations issued a risk alert noting it has seen investment companies and advisors mischaracterize their use of ESG factors in advertising or fund disclosures over the past four years 8. The message to the industry was unambiguous: the ESG label must correspond to actual investment practice.

7. The Proxy Advisor Duopoly: How ISS and Glass Lewis Shape ESG Fund Voting

7.1 Market Dominance

No analysis of ESG fund flows and performance is complete without examining the proxy advisory duopoly that effectively controls how institutional investors vote on ESG-related shareholder resolutions.

Institutional Shareholder Services (ISS) and Glass Lewis collectively control over 97% of the proxy advisory market as of 2024 11: - ISS: Approximately 48–52% market share - Glass Lewis: Approximately 42–45% market share - Combined assets under advice: More than $40 trillion 11

These two private firms, both foreign-owned, advise the world’s largest institutional investors — BlackRock, Vanguard, State Street, and thousands of pension funds — on how to vote on every shareholder resolution, including ESG-related proposals.

7.2 The ESG Voting Power

Proxy advisors’ influence on ESG outcomes is substantial. Their voting recommendations typically determine how institutional investors vote, and institutional investors own the majority of shares in most large public companies. When ISS or Glass Lewis recommends supporting an ESG shareholder proposal, that proposal’s passage probability increases dramatically.

However, ISS’s support for sustainability-related shareholder resolutions fell dramatically from 39% in 2024 to just 7% in 2025 11. This collapse in support reflected both political pressure and a reassessment of which ESG proposals were material to shareholder value.

7.3 Political Backlash and the Trump Executive Order

On December 11, 2025, President Trump signed an executive order explicitly targeting ISS and Glass Lewis, accusing the firms of using their market share “to advance and prioritize radical politically-motivated agendas” like DEI and ESG 11.

The EO directed the SEC, Department of Labor, and Federal Trade Commission to: - Review all rules and regulations related to proxy advisors - Consider whether proxy advisors should be classified as registered investment advisers - Assess potential antitrust violations - Require additional disclosures on ESG and DEI voting recommendations - Consider revising Rule 14a-8, which governs the shareholder proposal process 11

“Unbeknownst to many Americans, two foreign-owned proxy advisors, Institutional Shareholder Services Inc. and Glass, Lewis & Co., LLC, play a significant role in shaping the policies and priorities of America’s largest companies through the shareholder voting process.” — Executive Order, December 11, 2025 11

7.4 The Conflict of Interest

The proxy advisory model contains inherent conflicts of interest. ISS and Glass Lewis are paid by institutional investors for voting advice, but they also provide consulting services to corporations seeking to improve their governance ratings — creating a potential incentive to generate governance “problems” that require corporate consulting solutions.

More fundamentally, the concentrated power of two firms advising on $40 trillion in assets raises antitrust concerns. When two entities effectively control how the majority of institutional capital votes on ESG matters, the diversity of ESG perspectives in corporate governance is structurally limited.

8. ESG Fund Fee Drag: How Higher Fees Erode Returns

8.1 The Gross vs. Net Expense Ratio Puzzle

The conventional wisdom holds that ESG funds are systematically more expensive than traditional funds. The data tells a more nuanced story — one that reveals the industry’s true fee extraction strategy.

Gross Expense Ratios (2024): - ESG funds: 1.48% average gross expense ratio - Conventional funds: 1.34% average gross expense ratio 12

The gross difference appears modest — 14 basis points. But after fee waivers are applied, the picture inverts:

Net Expense Ratios (2024): - ESG funds: 0.85% average net expense ratio - Conventional funds: 1.01% average net expense ratio 12

ESG funds appear cheaper after waivers. This is because asset managers offer substantially larger fee waivers for ESG funds — averaging 0.63% versus only 0.33% for traditional funds 12.

8.2 The Loss-Leader Strategy

Why do asset managers waive more fees on ESG funds? The waivers serve as loss-leaders to attract institutional capital into ESG strategies, where the real profits are made through ancillary services — ESG data subscriptions, custom reporting, consulting mandates, and stewardship services 12.

The Fee Stacking Ecosystem: 1. Fund management fee: The visible tip (0.85% net) 2. ESG data subscriptions: Up to $487,000 annually for asset managers to process external ESG data 13 3. Custom ESG reporting: Additional fees for bespoke sustainability reports 4. Stewardship services: Proxy voting advice, engagement tracking 5. Consulting mandates: ESG strategy, materiality analysis ($20,000–$300,000+ annually) 13

The fund management fee is merely the entry point. Once an institutional client commits to an ESG strategy, the vendor lock-in across data, reporting, and consulting creates recurring revenue streams far exceeding the original fund fee.

8.3 The European Market

In European markets tracked by Morningstar, the average ESG fund charged 0.82%, slightly undercutting the 0.90% average for conventional funds 12. In the ETF space, the gap is narrowest: a traditional S&P 500 ETF charges as little as 0.03% (Vanguard VOO), while its ESG equivalent (Vanguard ESGV) charges 0.09% 14.

8.4 The Fee Drag on Returns

Even modest fee differentials compound dramatically over time. Consider an investor with a $100,000 portfolio earning 7% annually:

Expense Ratio Value After 20 Years Fees Paid
0.03% (traditional ETF) $386,968 $11,700
0.09% (ESG ETF) $382,947 $15,721
0.83% (active ESG) $326,438 $72,230
1.48% (gross ESG) $283,793 $114,875

An investor in a high-fee active ESG fund pays nearly $100,000 more in fees over 20 years compared to a low-cost traditional ETF — a gap that any ESG “outperformance” would need to overcome just to break even.

9. Evidence Table: ESG Fund Claims vs. Verified Performance Data

Claim Source Evidence Verdict
“ESG assets will reach $53 trillion by 2025” Bloomberg Intelligence (2021) 1 Actual: $3.9 trillion. Miss by $49.1 trillion (92.6%) False
“ESG funds consistently outperform” Industry marketing (2019–2022) Fraser Institute: “no reliable statistical relationship” 3 False
“ESG funds deliver risk-adjusted alpha” Northern Trust 4 “Do not perform better or worse” than non-ESG funds False
“Sustainable funds returned 12.6% in 2023” Morgan Stanley 5 True for 2023; narrowed to 0.6% in H1 2024 Partially True / Declining
“ESG outperformance survives controls” Stanford GSB 3 “Largely disappears” when retroactive biases removed False
“1,450+ EU funds rebranded” Morningstar/ESMA 9 31% of in-scope funds dropped ESG terms Verified
“$84 billion in 2025 outflows” Morningstar 6 Global net outflows; reversal from 2024 inflows Verified
“Invesco integrated ESG into 70–94% of assets” Invesco marketing (2020–2022) SEC: “lacked any written policy defining ESG integration” 8 False
“WisdomTree funds excluded fossil fuels” WisdomTree prospectus Funds held coal, natural gas, and tobacco securities 10 False
“ESG funds are more expensive” Conventional wisdom Net expense ratios actually lower due to fee waivers 12 Misleading
“ESG fee waivers benefit investors” Industry claim Waivers are loss-leaders for ancillary fee extraction 12 Partially True

10. The Regulatory Response: A Tightening Noose

The regulatory environment for ESG funds has shifted dramatically from accommodation to skepticism:

United States: - SEC disbanded its Climate and ESG Task Force in September 2024 — but ESG enforcement continued unabated 8 - SEC’s Division of Examinations identified ESG mischaracterization as a major examination priority 8 - SEC Names Rule requires fund names to accurately reflect investment focus 15 - President Trump’s December 2025 EO explicitly targeted proxy advisors’ ESG influence 11

European Union: - ESMA’s fund naming guidelines forced 1,450+ funds to rebrand or comply 9 - SFDR Article 8/9 classifications face ongoing scrutiny and potential revision - Stricter thresholds for what constitutes a “sustainable” investment

The net effect: the regulatory free-for-all that allowed funds to slap “ESG” on any product is ending. Funds must now substantiate their claims or face enforcement action.

Related Content

Chapter Title Connection
Chapter 1 The ESG Industrial Complex Examines the broader fee extraction ecosystem — consulting, ratings, and data — that creates the incentive for funds to market ESG products regardless of actual differentiation
Chapter 2 The Rating Agency Racket Documents how ESG rating divergence enables funds to construct portfolios with arbitrary “ESG” criteria, undermining claims of systematic outperformance
Chapter 5 Green Bonds and the Greenium Myth Analyzes the fixed-income parallel to ESG equity funds — how the “greenium” in bond markets transfers value from investors to issuers
Chapter 9 Regulatory Whiplash Covers the full scope of anti-ESG political backlash and regulatory reversal that drove the 2025 fund outflows and proxy advisor executive order

Known Limitations

AUM Measurement Variance: Different sources use different definitions of “ESG AUM” — from broad ESG-integrated mandates to dedicated sustainable funds. The $3.9 trillion figure uses Morningstar’s definition of dedicated sustainable funds; broader definitions yield higher numbers but capture funds with minimal ESG differentiation.

Performance Data Selection Bias: Academic studies on ESG performance vary in their methodology, time periods, and geographic coverage. The studies cited here represent the most rigorous meta-analyses available but cannot control for all confounding variables.

Proxy Advisor Data Opacity: ISS and Glass Lewis do not publicly disclose detailed voting recommendation data by category. Market share estimates are derived from industry reports and may not reflect 2026 conditions following the Trump EO.

Survivorship and Backfill Bias: Historical ESG index data suffers from known biases that may inflate apparent historical performance. The magnitude of this effect is debated among academics.

Rapidly Evolving Regulatory Environment: The regulatory framework for ESG funds is changing rapidly. The SEC Names Rule, ESMA guidelines, and the Trump EO on proxy advisors all represent significant developments that may alter fund behavior and AUM calculations going forward.

Confidential Settlements: SEC settlements with Invesco and WisdomTree were made without admission or denial of wrongdoing. The factual allegations represent the SEC’s claims, not judicial findings.

14: NerdWallet, “14 Best-Performing and Cheap ESG Funds for 2026” URL: https://www.nerdwallet.com/investing/learn/best-esg-funds Date: July 2026 Excerpt: “Vanguard ESGV charges 0.09%” vs. “Vanguard VOO at 0.03%”; “actively managed ESG funds average around 0.83%” Context: Consumer-facing fee comparison data for ETF and mutual fund ESG products. Confidence: MEDIUM-HIGH — NerdWallet aggregates from fund prospectuses.

15: Holland & Knight, “SEC Initiates Review of ESG Fund Names Rule” URL: https://www.hollandknight.com/insights/sec-initiates-review-esg-fund-names-rule Date: March 2026 Excerpt: “SEC initiated review of its ESG Fund Names Rule, requiring fund names to accurately reflect investment focus” Context: Legal analysis of regulatory tightening on ESG fund naming. Confidence: HIGH — Law firm analysis of SEC regulatory action.

Chapter 3 — The Gap Between Marketing and Returns Word count: ~4,850 Citations: 15 numbered sources (30+ underlying primary references) Evidence table: 11 claims evaluated Last updated: July 2026

Chapter 4: Carbon Credit Markets — Fraud, Double-Counting, and Phantom Offsets

Research Date: July 2026 Searches Conducted: 20+ independent queries Sources Cited: 25+ primary sources Confidence Level: High Temporal Caveat: Research current as of July 2026. Carbon credit markets are evolving rapidly, with new investigations, regulatory actions, and methodological reforms emerging continuously.

The Theater of Carbon Neutrality

In January 2023, a joint investigation by The Guardian, Die Zeit, and SourceMaterial dropped a bomb on the voluntary carbon market. After nine months of forensic analysis, the investigators concluded that 94% of rainforest carbon offsets certified by Verra — the world’s largest carbon credit certifier — were worthless “phantom credits” with no climate benefit whatsoever 28. Major global brands including Disney, Shell, Gucci, and Salesforce had purchased these credits and used them to market their products and operations as environmentally sustainable 28. The revelation was not merely embarrassing. It exposed the voluntary carbon market as a multi-billion-dollar apparatus in which corporations bought the appearance of climate action while the atmosphere received nothing in return.

This chapter traces the money through every stage of the carbon credit supply chain — from project developers in developing countries to certifiers, brokers, traders, and ultimately to corporate buyers seeking carbon neutrality claims. The picture is one of systemic dysfunction: inflated baselines, double-counting, fraudulent certifications, broker markups of 300–700%, and a regulatory vacuum where no enforcement mechanism exists.

The voluntary carbon market functions as a wealth transfer mechanism — moving money from corporate buyers to intermediaries while delivering phantom environmental benefits.

Market Size: The $4 Billion to $17 Billion Guess

No one knows precisely how large the voluntary carbon market is — that opacity is itself a red flag.

Market size estimates vary by a factor of more than four, reflecting the market’s structural lack of transparency:

Source Estimated Size (2024–2025) Projection
Grand View Research $4.04 billion 35.1% CAGR through 2030
Mordor Intelligence $15.83 billion (2025) $120.47 billion by 2030
Market Research Future $16.72 billion (2025) N/A
MSCI $1.4 billion in credits actually used (2024) Below 2022 peak

The MSCI figure — $1.4 billion in credits actually used by corporations in 2024 — is perhaps the most telling, as it measures actual credit retirement rather than total market turnover 27. The wide variance between estimates reflects the market’s reliance on over-the-counter transactions with no public price discovery, no standardized contracts, and no mandatory reporting. Morgan Stanley has projected the voluntary carbon market could grow from approximately $2 billion in 2022 to roughly $100 billion by decade’s end 27. Whether that growth materializes depends entirely on whether the market can solve its credibility crisis.

The voluntary carbon market’s opacity is not accidental. It is structural. Most transactions occur through bilateral negotiations or unlisted contracts, making price discovery impossible for buyers and enabling the extraordinary fee extraction by intermediaries documented later in this chapter 11.

The Verra/Guardian Investigation: 94% Phantom Credits

The Bombshell

In January 2023, The Guardian, Die Zeit, and SourceMaterial published the results of a nine-month joint investigation into Verra, the world’s leading voluntary carbon credit certifier 28. The findings were devastating:

94% of rainforest carbon offsets certified by Verra were likely “phantom credits” — certifications for emissions reductions that never actually occurred 28

The threat of deforestation had been overstated by approximately 400% on average for Verra-approved projects 28

Of 87 projects analyzed, only a handful demonstrated any actual reduction in deforestation 28

A flagship REDD+ project in Peru involved forced evictions and destruction of residents’ homes 28

The methodology was rigorous. The investigation combined satellite imagery analysis, on-the-ground reporting, and comparison of Verra-certified projects against independent academic baselines. The investigators did not cherry-pick failing projects. They examined the certifier’s entire portfolio of rainforest protection credits — the single largest category in the voluntary carbon market.

The Corporate Buyers

The investigation revealed that some of the world’s most recognizable brands had built marketing campaigns around these worthless credits. Disney used rainforest offsets to claim environmental responsibility for its operations and cruises. Shell purchased credits as part of its “Drive Carbon Neutral” program. Gucci marketed products as carbon-neutral based on offset purchases. Salesforce incorporated the credits into its corporate sustainability reporting 28.

None of these companies had independently verified the credits they purchased. They relied on Verra’s certification as sufficient evidence of environmental benefit — a reliance that the investigation proved was misplaced.

Verra’s Response and Collateral Damage

Verra forcefully contested the investigation’s methodology, arguing that the academic approaches used by investigators did not accurately assess deforestation prevention 28. The organization claimed that its methodologies were sound and that the comparison periods used by investigators were too short to capture the full protective effect of certified projects.

But the damage was done. Verra announced plans to phase out its legacy rainforest protection methodologies — an implicit admission that existing standards were inadequate 28. CEO David Antonioli ultimately resigned 28. The world’s largest carbon credit certifier had been forced into retreat.

The Guardian investigation catalyzed a broader reassessment of the voluntary carbon market and contributed to regulatory scrutiny on both sides of the Atlantic.

The Money Trail: Corporations purchased phantom credits for public relations and, increasingly, for regulatory compliance. The money flowed from corporate buyers in developed countries to project developers (often in the Global South) to certifiers like Verra. Every participant in the chain profited — except the climate, which received no benefit. The total value of these worthless credits ran into the hundreds of millions of dollars 28.

Kenneth Newcombe: When Carbon Credit Fraud Becomes Criminal

The Indictment

In October 2024, Kenneth Newcombe — former CEO of C-Quest Capital and former member of Verra’s board of directors — was indicted by U.S. federal prosecutors in the Southern District of New York for a multi-million dollar carbon credit fraud scheme 29. The charges represented a watershed moment: carbon credit fraud had moved from journalistic exposure to criminal prosecution.

Newcombe and his associates were accused of a multi-year scheme to fraudulently obtain carbon credits using manipulated data 29. The scheme involved clean cooking stove projects in developing countries — projects claiming to reduce emissions by replacing traditional biomass-burning stoves. Prosecutors alleged the emission reductions were fabricated or dramatically inflated.

Simultaneously, the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) brought parallel civil charges against Newcombe, describing the case as the first carbon market fraud prosecution in the commission’s history 30. Newcombe faced charges of wire fraud and commodities fraud, carrying potential sentences of up to decades in federal prison 31.

Why It Matters

The Newcombe case was significant for three reasons beyond the specific allegations:

First, Newcombe was not a marginal player. As a former Verra board member, he had helped shape the very certification standards that C-Quest Capital’s projects were evaluated against. His alleged involvement in fraud raised serious questions about whether the certification system itself had been compromised from within 29.

Second, the case demonstrated that U.S. law enforcement was prepared to treat carbon credits as commodities subject to fraud prosecution. The CFTC’s involvement signaled that carbon markets would not remain the enforcement-free zone they had been for two decades 30.

Third, the clean cooking stove sector — which the Newcombe case targeted — had been widely promoted as one of the most promising categories of carbon offsets, with strong co-benefits for public health and gender equality. If even these “gold standard” projects were fraudulent, the implications for the broader market were severe 31.

The Money Trail: C-Quest Capital sold carbon credits to corporate buyers and investors. The indictment alleged that these credits were generated using falsified data about stove usage and emission reductions. Every credit sold represented revenue for C-Quest and its principals — revenue obtained through what prosecutors alleged was systematic fraud 29.

REDD+ Project Failures: Systemic Collapse of Forest Conservation Credits

REDD+ (Reducing Emissions from Deforestation and Forest Degradation) represents the single largest category of nature-based carbon credits. It is also the most systematically flawed.

The Four Fatal Defects

REDD+ projects suffer from four interlocking structural problems that make meaningful verification virtually impossible 32:

1. Inflated Baselines. REDD+ projects claim they prevent deforestation that would have occurred without the project’s intervention. This “counterfactual” baseline is inherently unobservable — it requires proving what would have happened in an alternate universe where the project did not exist. Multiple studies have found that project developers systematically inflate these baselines, leading to credits for emissions reductions that were never at risk of occurring 32. A 2023 study published in Science found that carbon offsets from REDD+ projects are “issued on the basis of comparison between observed forest cover and inflated counterfactual scenarios” 34.

2. Carbon Leakage. Protecting one forest frequently shifts deforestation to an adjacent, unprotected area. If a REDD+ project prevents logging in one valley, the logging demand does not disappear — it simply moves to the next valley, the next country, or the next continent. The net effect on atmospheric carbon may be zero or even negative 32.

3. Additionality Failure. The “additionality” test asks whether a project would have proceeded without carbon credit revenue. If a forest was never at serious risk of being cut down, then selling credits for “protecting” it generates revenue for nothing. A 2019 ProPublica investigation found that “almost all of the projects failed to meet a standard required for any true carbon offset called additionality” 33.

4. The Structural Absurdity. Even if a REDD+ project perfectly protects a forest, purchasing its credit to “offset” ongoing emissions does not remove greenhouse gases from the atmosphere. It merely maintains the status quo — preventing additional emissions rather than reducing existing concentrations. This is not carbon removal. It is carbon avoidance, and it cannot mathematically neutralize ongoing fossil fuel combustion 32.

The Academic Verdict

A major study from the Oxford Smith School of Enterprise and the Environment concluded in 2024 that “carbon offsets have failed” over 25 years and “most should be phased out” 32. This was not a fringe position. It was the assessment of one of the world’s leading climate economics research centers, based on decades of evidence.

The ProPublica investigation reached an equally damning conclusion years earlier. Reporter Lisa Song examined forest preservation projects across Brazil, Indonesia, and other countries and found that almost none delivered the carbon benefits they claimed 33.

The Money Trail: REDD+ projects generated hundreds of millions of dollars in credit sales. The money flowed from corporate buyers in North America and Europe to project developers in rainforest nations, with certifiers and brokers extracting fees at every stage. The forests, in many cases, received protection that was illusory — either because they were never at risk or because the protection simply shifted deforestation elsewhere 32.

Double-Counting: Selling the Same Tonne Three Times

Double-counting is not a bug in carbon markets. It is a feature of the system’s architecture.

How Double-Counting Works

The fundamental mechanism is simple: a single emissions reduction is claimed by multiple parties simultaneously 35. The most common pathway occurs when a developing country hosts a carbon project to meet its own Paris Agreement Nationally Determined Contribution (NDC) while simultaneously selling the same credit to an international buyer. Both parties claim the same tonne of CO2 reduced toward their respective climate targets 35.

Consider a wind farm in Kenya. Kenya may count the wind farm’s emissions reductions toward its national climate pledge under the Paris Agreement. Simultaneously, a European airline may purchase credits from the same wind farm to claim carbon-neutral flights. The same megawatt-hour of clean electricity is counted twice — once by Kenya, once by the airline 35.

The Paris Agreement’s Article 6 established frameworks to prevent this through “corresponding adjustments” — requiring countries to deduct sold credits from their own inventories. But implementation has been slow, inconsistent, and riddled with exceptions. Enforcement mechanisms remain virtually nonexistent 35.

The Honor System Problem

Beyond double-counting across jurisdictions, the verification system itself operated for years on what can only be described as an honor system. Project developers self-reported data about their emission reductions. Third-party auditors checked whether the reports conformed to registry methodologies — but did not independently assess whether the claimed emissions reductions had actually occurred 35.

This created a predictable incentive structure: project developers who reported favorable data received more credits, generating more revenue. Auditors who were too rigorous risked losing business to more accommodating competitors. The entire system was designed for convenience rather than accuracy.

Reform Attempts

Major registries — Verra, Gold Standard, and the American Carbon Registry — have been tightening their methodologies in response to mounting criticism. The Integrity Council for the Voluntary Carbon Market (ICVCM) introduced Core Carbon Principles (CCPs) in an attempt to establish baseline quality standards 35. Whether these reforms are sufficient remains contested. Critics argue the ICVCM lacks enforcement power and its principles are too lenient to exclude flawed project types like REDD+.

Phantom Offsets: Credits for Nothing

Phantom offsets are credits issued for emissions reductions that never occurred — whether because the underlying project was fictitious, the baseline was inflated, or the project would have proceeded without carbon credit revenue.

The Scale of the Problem

The Verra investigation found that up to 90% of rainforest offset credits from leading certifiers offered no real emissions reductions 35. This is not a marginal failure rate. It is wholesale market dysfunction.

The mechanisms producing phantom offsets include:

Fictitious Projects: Credits issued for projects that do not exist or never became operational. A developer submits a project design document, receives certification, sells credits — and the wind turbines, cookstoves, or conservation areas are never actually built or maintained.

Inflated Baselines: The most common mechanism. Project developers claim that without their intervention, deforestation or emissions would have been far worse than any credible scenario suggests. The difference between the inflated baseline and the actual (modest) outcome generates millions of phantom credits 28.

Non-Additional Projects: Credits sold for projects that would have happened anyway — often because they were legally required, already profitable, or fully funded through other means. A hydropower dam mandated by national law does not become “additional” simply because its developer also sells carbon credits 33.

The Peru Project: Credits Built on Forced Evictions

The Verra investigation uncovered a particularly egregious case: a flagship REDD+ project in Peru that not only failed to deliver genuine carbon benefits but was implicated in forced evictions and destruction of residents’ homes 28. Indigenous communities were allegedly driven from their land to create the appearance of a protected forest — a forest that was then used to generate carbon credits sold to multinational corporations.

This case illustrates a moral dimension often overlooked in technical discussions of carbon markets: the people most directly affected by carbon credit projects — frequently Indigenous and rural communities in the Global South — bear the costs while intermediaries in London, New York, and Singapore capture the profits 28.

Broker Fee Extraction: The 700% Markup

If the project developers and certifiers are the manufacturers of carbon credits, the brokers are the retailers — and their margins would make luxury goods dealers blush.

The Opacity Premium

The voluntary carbon market’s extreme opacity creates extraordinary profit opportunities for intermediaries. Investigations by SourceMaterial, Carbon Market Watch, and Greenpeace Unearthed revealed a consistent pattern 11:

Brokers purchase credits from project developers at low prices and resell them at markups of 300% to 700% 11

In one documented case, brokers purchased forestry offsets at low prices and sold them at a 700% premium while falsely claiming that up to 95% of the money went to project owners 11

The average broker commission is 15.5% per transaction, in addition to hidden wholesale margins that are not disclosed to buyers 11

Most transactions occur over-the-counter through unlisted contracts, making price discovery impossible for buyers 11

The Funnel Effect

The carbon credit supply chain resembles a funnel where value concentrates at the top:

Project developers in developing countries receive a small fraction of the final sale price — often just 10–30% of credit value 11

Verifiers and certifiers take their fees for validating projects — typically 5–15% 11

Brokers and traders extract the largest margins through opaque pricing — 300–700% markups 11

Corporate buyers purchase peace of mind and marketing material at enormous markups 11

The climate receives whatever benefit is left — frequently nothing 28

SourceMaterial’s investigation concluded: “Payments for carbon offsets routinely end up in the hands of middlemen instead of the conservationists they are designed to fund” 11. Greenpeace Unearthed reached the same finding through independent reporting 36.

The Digital Marketplace Response

New platforms like Carbonmark and AirCarbon Exchange have emerged to provide transparent, on-chain pricing with publicly listed transaction fees — directly addressing the opacity problem 11. Whether these platforms can capture sufficient market share to discipline the broader market remains uncertain. The incumbent brokers have deep relationships with corporate buyers and little incentive to embrace transparency.

The Money Trail: A carbon credit that costs $2 to produce in a developing country can be sold to a corporate buyer for $15–20 after passing through two or three intermediaries. The broker who never set foot in the rainforest captures more value than the community that actually maintains the forest 11.

Corporate Carbon Neutrality: Marketing on Sand

The entire edifice of carbon credit fraud rests on corporate demand. Without buyers willing to purchase credits and claim carbon neutrality, there would be no market for phantom offsets. The corporate sector has been not merely a participant but an enabler — and in many cases, a knowing one.

The Consumer Facing Claims

The Verra investigation documented that Disney used rainforest offsets to market its cruises and theme parks as environmentally responsible 28. Shell sold gasoline under a “Drive Carbon Neutral” banner, suggesting that purchasing their fuel could be climate-neutral thanks to offset purchases. Gucci marketed products as carbon-neutral. Salesforce incorporated offsets into corporate sustainability reports used to attract ESG-focused investors 28.

These were not obscure brands making modest claims. They were some of the world’s most valuable companies, leveraging carbon credits to differentiate their products, attract environmentally conscious consumers, and meet sustainability commitments made to investors and regulators.

The Due Diligence Failure

None of these companies conducted independent verification of the credits they purchased. They relied on Verra’s certification mark as sufficient evidence of environmental benefit — a reliance that proved catastrophically misplaced 28.

The due diligence failure extends beyond individual companies to the entire ESG ratings and data infrastructure. ESG rating agencies that scored companies favorably based on carbon neutrality claims did not verify those claims. Investment funds that marketed themselves as sustainable accepted offset-based neutrality at face value. Auditors providing assurance on sustainability reports treated purchased credits as genuine emissions reductions 28.

The Incentive Structure

Why did sophisticated corporations fail to detect worthless credits? The answer lies in incentives. For a sustainability officer under pressure to hit a carbon neutrality target, purchasing cheap offsets is far easier than reducing operational emissions. For a marketing department, “carbon neutral” is a powerful claim regardless of its validity. For executives with compensation tied to ESG metrics, offset purchases are the path of least resistance 28.

The voluntary carbon market did not fail despite corporate demand. It failed because of it — demand for the appearance of climate action rather than the reality.

Regulatory Gaps: The Enforcement Vacuum

Perhaps the most striking feature of the voluntary carbon market is not the fraud, the phantom credits, or the broker markups. It is the near-total absence of regulation.

No Standardization

Unlike financial markets, which operate under standardized contracts, disclosure requirements, and enforcement mechanisms, the voluntary carbon market is a patchwork of private registries with inconsistent methodologies and no government oversight 35. Verra, Gold Standard, and the American Carbon Registry set their own rules, certify their own projects, and face no external accountability when those certifications prove worthless 28.

No Enforcement

Until the Newcombe indictment in October 2024, no senior carbon market participant had faced criminal prosecution for credit fraud in the United States 29. The CFTC had never brought a carbon market fraud case 30. Civil litigation against credit sellers was rare and typically settled confidentially. The result was a market in which the downside of selling fraudulent credits was negligible and the upside was enormous 29.

The CFTC’s New Role

The Newcombe case signaled a potential shift. By characterizing carbon credits as commodities subject to the Commodity Exchange Act, the CFTC asserted jurisdiction over carbon credit fraud for the first time 30. This opened the door to future prosecutions and created a deterrent effect that had not previously existed.

But the CFTC’s jurisdiction has limits. It covers fraud in the sale of carbon credits, not the underlying environmental validity of certified projects. A credit can be sold without fraud — with full disclosure — and still represent no genuine emissions reduction. The CFTC can prosecute liars; it cannot prosecute sellers of well-documented phantom credits 30.

International Coordination Failures

The voluntary carbon market is global, but regulation is national and fragmented. A project certified in one country, sold through brokers in a second, and retired by a buyer in a third falls into a jurisdictional no-man’s land. No international body has the authority to investigate, prosecute, or penalize market manipulation across borders 35.

The ICVCM’s Core Carbon Principles represent an attempt at self-regulation, but the ICVCM has no enforcement power. It can approve or disapprove methodologies, but it cannot fine participants, revoke credits, or compel restitution 35.

Evidence Table: Carbon Credit Claims vs. Verified Outcomes

Evidence Supporting Market Integrity Evidence Challenging Market Integrity Source Assessment
ICVCM Core Carbon Principles establish baseline quality standards for approved credits 35 94% of Verra rainforest offsets found worthless by independent investigation 28 The CCPs are new, untested, and lack enforcement power. The 94% figure comes from a rigorous nine-month joint investigation by three reputable outlets.
Major registries (Verra, Gold Standard) have committed to tightening methodologies 35 Oxford Smith School concluded “carbon offsets have failed” over 25 years; “most should be phased out” 32 Registry reforms are reactive and incomplete. The Oxford conclusion is based on decades of peer-reviewed research.
CFTC prosecution of Kenneth Newcombe demonstrates emerging enforcement 30 ProPublica found “almost all” forest projects failed the additionality test 33 The Newcombe case is a single prosecution in a multi-billion-dollar market. The ProPublica finding was based on extensive field reporting.
Digital marketplaces (Carbonmark, AirCarbon) promise greater transparency 11 Brokers routinely extract 300–700% markups through opaque pricing 11 New platforms are small and have not yet achieved market dominance. The markup data is well-documented.
Some carbon removal technologies (direct air capture, biochar) offer measurable outcomes REDD+ credits based on unobservable counterfactual baselines are inherently unverifiable 32 Carbon removal is distinct from carbon avoidance/offsetting. REDD+ structural problems may be intractable.
MSCI data shows $1.4B in credits actually used in 2024 — some genuine demand exists 27 Science journal found REDD+ credits issued on “inflated counterfactual scenarios” 34 Credit retirement does not prove environmental validity. The Science finding directly challenges the measurement basis of forest credits.

Related Content

This chapter connects directly to several other investigations in this volume:

Chapter 2 — “The ESG Rating Racket: How MSCI, Sustainalytics, and S&P Sell Influence”: Examines how ESG rating agencies provided favorable scores to companies purchasing worthless carbon credits, and how the ESG data oligopoly profited from sustainability reporting demands.

Chapter 5 — “Green Bonds: When the Color of Money Doesn’t Change Its Impact”: Investigates the parallel market for green debt instruments, where “greenium” pricing and second-party opinion conflicts of interest create similar opportunities for value extraction without environmental delivery.

Chapter 9 — “The Consulting-Industrial Complex: McKinsey, BCG, and the $50 Billion Climate Advice Machine”: Documents how the same consulting firms advising corporations on carbon neutrality strategies were simultaneously selling carbon credit brokerage and verification services — creating conflicts of interest throughout the supply chain.

Chapter 13 — “Indigenous Land, Corporate Gain: The Extraction Behind Conservation Credits”: Provides deeper reporting on the human cost of carbon credit projects, including the forced evictions documented in Verra-certified REDD+ projects and the asymmetry of benefits between project-affected communities and credit buyers.
